1: 1 Chronicles begins with nine chapters of genealogies (chs. 1-9) — from Adam to the post-exilic community. The genealogical prologue serves a theological purpose: to demonstrate the continuity of YHWH's covenant people through the Babylonian exile. The lists trace: Adam to Israel (ch. 1), the twelve tribes (chs. 2-9), with special focus on the line of David (ch. 3, which includes the post-exilic Davidic line down to the 6th generation after Zerubbabel — into the 5th century BCE). Matthew's genealogy (Matt 1:1-17) is a direct descendant of the Chronicler's method: beginning with Abraham, structured in three sets of fourteen, it traces the covenant line to the Messiah through the same Davidic focus that Chronicles establishes.
2: The three names — Kenan, Mahalalel, Jared (qênān mah˘l˘lʾêl yereḏ) — continue the asyndetic list: names only, no ‘begat’ verb. In Gen 5:9-20 each patriarch has a full entry with lifespan and the wayyôled formula. The Chronicler strips all that away — maximum genealogical compression. What Genesis takes three verses per name, Chronicles covers in one verse. The purpose is lineage, not biography: the claim that this thread of human beings leads unbroken to Israel and David.
3: Enoch, Methuselah, Lamech (ĥnôḵ mêtûšelaḥ lāmeḵ) — the three pre-Flood names before Noah. Enoch is theologically significant in Gen 5:24 (‘God took him’) and Heb 11:5 (translated by faith without seeing death), but the Chronicler omits this. The genealogy is purely structural: names as markers of the chain toward David, not occasions for biographical comment.
4: nôaḥ stands alone; the sentence then shifts: ‘The sons of Noah: Shem, Ham, and Japheth’ (bênê-nôaḥ šêm ĥām wāyepet). The syntactic break from name-only list to the explicit sons-of formula marks the major division point. At Noah the genealogy branches; what follows in vv5-23 is the Table of Nations (Gen 10 compressed). The break also signals theological weight: the flood divides primordial history from the nations, and the Shem line will carry the covenant forward.
5: The seven sons of Japheth (bênê yepet): Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, Tiras. Identical to Gen 10:2. The Japheth branch is listed first (eldest son), though the covenant passes through Shem. Ezek 38–39 uses Gog, Magog, Tubal, and Meshech as the great northern adversary — the Chronicler’s naming them grounds them within YHWH’s created order, not outside it.
6: Sons of Gomer (bênê gômer): Ashkenaz, Riphath, Togarmah. In Jer 51:27 Ashkenaz is summoned to fight against Babylon. The names of Gen 10 are not merely ancient ethnic labels but geo-political realities in the Chronicler’s world — he writes knowing that these table-of-nations names map onto known peoples and territories.
7: Sons of Javan (bênê yāwān): Elishah, Tarshish, Kittim, Dodanim. Javan = Ionia (Greece/Aegean). Kittim = Cyprus or the western Mediterranean more broadly; in Dan 11:30 ‘ships of Kittim’ refers to Rome. Tarshish is the far western sea-route (Jonah 1:3; Ps 48:7). The Japheth branch charts the northern and western peoples.
8: Sons of Ham (bênê ĥām): Cush, Egypt (miṣrayim), Put, Canaan. miṣrayim is the standard Hebrew dual-form name for Egypt, referring to Upper and Lower Egypt. The Ham branch is theologically loaded for Israel: Canaan is the land Israel occupied; Egypt is the land of slavery; Cush is the region to Egypt’s south. These names frame the nations Israel will encounter as part of the single human family descended from Noah.
9: Sons of Cush (bênê kûš): Seba, Havilah, Sabta, Raamah, Sabteca; sons of Raamah: Sheba and Dedan. The Cush branch extends into southern Arabia and East Africa. Sheba appears in 2 Chr 9 (the queen of Sheba visits Solomon) — one of the distant nations that comes to honor the Davidic king. The genealogical root of Sheba in Cush’s line traces her people back to Noah.
10: wayyôleḏ kûš ʾet-nimrôḏ hûʾ hêḥêl lihyôt gibbôr bāʾāreṣ — ‘Cush fathered Nimrod; he was the first to be a mighty man on earth.’ The verbal form hêḥêl (began, Hiphil of ĥālal) + infinitive construct lihyôt signals inception: Nimrod was the initiator of a type. gibbôr (mighty one, champion) is the same term used for the primordial Nephilim (gibbôrîm) in Gen 6:4 — figures of ambiguous human greatness before the flood. Chronicles drops Gen 10:9’s qualifier ‘mighty hunter before YHWH’ and the proverb ‘like Nimrod, a mighty hunter before YHWH’ — focusing purely on his status as the first gibbôr. The city-building tradition (Gen 10:10-12, founding Babylon and Nineveh) is also omitted; the Chronicler streamlines to lineage.
11: miṣrayim fathered the Ludites, Anamites, Lehabites, Naphtuhites. These are gentilics — collective nouns for peoples derived from eponymous ancestors. The Hebrew form ending in -îm (plural) is the marker: lûḏîm, ʿănāmîm, etc. The ancestors are individuals; the descendants are peoples. The Hebrew reader understands that these names refer to nations, not persons.
12: Pathrusites (patrûsîm, from Pathros = Upper Egypt), Casluhites (kaslûḥîm) — ‘from whom the Philistines came’ (ʾăšer yāṣĕʾû miššāmm pêeštîm), and Caphtorites. The parenthetical identification of the Philistines’ origin inserts a politically charged note: the Philistines, Israel’s persistent enemy in the Davidic narrative (Chronicles focuses on David’s Philistine victories), are placed within the same genealogical structure as Israel. Amos 9:7: ‘Did I not bring the Philistines from Caphtor?’ — all ultimately under YHWH’s governance.
13: Canaan fathered Sidon (ṣîḏôn bêḵôrô, ‘his firstborn’) and Heth. bêḵôr (firstborn) is the only birth-order designation in the Ham genealogy — Sidon’s primacy in the Canaanite branch is noted. Heth is the ancestor of the Hittites; Abraham buys the cave of Machpelah from Ephron the Hittite (Gen 23), grounding the patriarchal land-purchase within this genealogical lineage.
14: The Jebusites (yêḇûsî), Amorites (ʾĕmôrî), Girgashites (girgāšî) — three of the seven Canaanite nations of Deut 7:1. The Jebusites are specifically the inhabitants of Jerusalem before David conquered it (2 Sam 5:6-9; 1 Chr 11:4-8) — the Chronicler who narrates David’s capture of Jerusalem places the Jebusite population within Noah’s grandson Canaan’s genealogy.
15: Hivites (ĥiwwî), Arkites (ʿarqî), Sinites (sînî). The Hivites are the people of Gibeon who deceived Joshua into a covenant (Josh 9); the covenant Joshua was tricked into became the template for Israelite treatment of Canaanites within the land. The genealogy places even Israel’s treaty-partner peoples within the Noahic framework.
16: Arvadites (ʾarwāḏî), Zemarites (ṣĕmārî), Hamathites (ĥămātî). Hamath is the northernmost city of the Canaanite region — ‘the entrance of Hamath’ is the standard northern boundary marker for the ideal extent of Israel’s territory (Num 34:8; 1 Kgs 8:65). The Chronicler closes the Canaan list at Hamath, bracketing the Canaanite genealogy within the promised land’s geographical limits.
17: Sons of Shem (bênê šêm): Elam, Asshur, Arphaxad, Lud, Aram — then Uz, Hul, Gether, and Meshech. The list in Gen 10:22 gives only five sons of Shem; the additional names (Uz, Hul, Gether, Meshech) appear in Gen 10:23 as sons of Aram, not Shem. Chronicles conflates them, placing Aram’s sons at the same level as Shem’s sons — possibly a textual transmission error or deliberate compression. The LXX of Chronicles reads similarly. This is a significant textual variant from the Genesis Vorlage: the Chronicler’s Shem-list is not a simple copy of Gen 10.
18: Arphaxad fathered Shelah, and Shelah fathered Eber (wayyôleḏ ʾarpaḵšaḏ ʾet-šelaḥ wĕšelaḥ yālaḏ ʾet-ʿēḇer). The introduction of the wayyôleḏ verb (narrative past: ‘and he fathered’) after the name-only lists of vv5-17 signals that the Arphaxad-Shelah-Eber line is the one that matters. The genealogy narrows structurally as it approaches the covenant ancestor: verbal narration replaces paratactic names.
19: Eber had two sons: the first was named Peleg (peleḡ), ‘because in his time the earth was divided’ (kî bêyāmāyw niplêḡāh hāʾāreṣ). peleḡ derives from the root pālaḡ (to divide, split). The paronomasia — naming a child after the event of his era — is standard Hebrew etymological convention. The phrase niplêḡāh hāʾāreṣ (the earth was divided, Niphal perfect) is usually interpreted as the dispersal of nations after Babel (Gen 11:1-9). The Chronicler preserves this note verbatim from Gen 10:25 without elaboration.
20: Joktan (yoqṭān) fathered Almodad, Sheleph, Hazarmaveth, Jerah — beginning the South Arabian branch. Several names correspond to known Arabian toponyms: Hazarmaveth = Hadramaut in Yemen. The Joktan branch (vv20-23) is a closed catalogue; the genealogy does not extend it beyond one generation. The covenant path through Peleg is where the narrative interest lies.
21: Hadoram, Uzal, Diklah — three more Joktan sons. Uzal is usually identified with Sana’a in Yemen (cf. Ezek 27:19). The place-names embedded in the genealogy provide the Chronicler’s audience with a mental map of the known world from the vantage of the ancient Near East.
22: Ebal, Abimael, Sheba — more Joktan sons. Sheba appears here as a Joktan descendant (South Arabian) and also in v9 as a Cushite (through Raamah). The double appearance of Sheba in both the Ham and Shem branches reflects either competing traditions the genealogy preserves without harmonizing, or the awareness that the ‘Sheba’ toponym covered multiple geographic/ethnic entities.
23: Ophir, Havilah, Jobab — final Joktan sons. The closing formula kol-ʾēlleh bĕnê yoqṭān (all these were the sons of Joktan) marks the end of a genealogical sub-unit. Ophir is the gold-source to which Solomon sent trading expeditions (1 Kgs 9:28; 1 Chr 29:4 ‘gold of Ophir’). Its placement in the Table of Nations grounds the Solomonic trade network in the created human geography — the distant lands that supply the temple treasures are part of the same Noahic family.
24: Shem, Arphaxad, Shelah (šêm ʾarpaḵšaḏ šālaḥ) — the asyndetic list resumes after the narrative notes of vv18-23, now tracing the line directly from Shem to Abraham. The use of the same paratactic style as vv1-3 signals a vertical genealogy (linear descent) rather than a branching one (horizontal). The Shem-to-Abraham spine is the structural backbone of all OT covenantal genealogy.
25: Eber, Peleg, Reu (ʿēḇer peleḡ rĕʿû) — continuing the direct descent line. Eber is the ancestor of the ʿiḇrîm (Hebrews); the connection between Eber and ‘Hebrew’ was recognized in ancient tradition (Gen 10:21 ‘Shem, the father of all the children of Eber’).
26: Serug, Nahor, Terah (śĕrûḡ nāĥôr teraḥ) — three generations before Abraham. Terah led the family from Ur of the Chaldeans toward Canaan but stopped at Haran (Gen 11:31). The brevity of the list (one name per verse) accelerates the reader toward the climax: Abraham.
27: ʾaḇrām hûʾ ʾaḇrāhām — ‘Abram, that is, Abraham.’ The identifying particle hûʾ functions as an equative marker: this person Abram is the one who became Abraham (Gen 17:5, where YHWH changes the name because ‘I have made you the father of a multitude of nations’ — ʾaḇ h˘môn gôyîm, a paronomasia on ʾaḇrāhām). The Chronicler signals the name-change without retelling the covenant of circumcision (Gen 17). The hûʾ identification also appears in v34 for Israel/Jacob — the Chronicler’s shorthand for covenant identity transition.
28: Sons of Abraham: Isaac and Ishmael (bênê ʾaḇrāhām yiṣḥāq wĕyišmāʿêl). The order (Isaac first, Ishmael second) reverses birth order — Ishmael is the elder (Gen 16) — to signal covenantal priority. In Hebrew genealogy, first position in a list connotes primary standing. Paul makes this argument explicitly in Gal 4:22-31 (the two sons of Abraham representing the two covenants).
29: Ishmael’s sons ‘in birth order’ (bêḵûrôtām): the firstborn Nebaioth, then Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam. bêḵûrôt is an abstract noun from bêḵôr (firstborn) meaning ‘sequence of birth.’ The specification that Ishmael’s sons are listed in birth order emphasizes that the list is complete and properly ordered. Kedar appears in prophetic texts (Isa 21:16-17; 42:11; 60:7) as a significant North Arabian tribal group.
30: Mishma, Dumah, Massa, Hadad, Tema — five more sons of Ishmael. Massa and Tema appear in Prov 30:1 and 31:1 as place-names associated with wisdom literature. The genealogy grounds the non-Israelite wisdom tradition (Proverbs draws on an international wisdom heritage) within the Abrahamic family.
31: Jetur, Naphish, Kedemah — final sons of Ishmael. ‘These were the sons of Ishmael’ (ʾēlleh hêm bênê yišmāʿêl) closes the Ishmael catalogue. Jetur and Naphish appear in 1 Chr 5:19-22 as peoples against whom the Transjordanian tribes of Reuben, Gad, and half-Manasseh fought successfully — the genealogical names in ch. 1 remain historically active in the Chronicler’s own narrative.
32: Sons of Keturah, Abraham’s concubine (pîlegeš): Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, Shuah. pîlegeš (concubine) is the precise term used in Gen 25:6 for Keturah’s status — secondary wife, not the primary covenant wife Sarah. Midian is the most significant: the Midianites are Israel’s enemy (Num 25; Judg 6-8), yet Moses’s father-in-law Jethro is a Midianite priest (Exod 2:16-18) — Abraham’s descendant and Israel’s adversary simultaneously.
33: Sons of Midian: Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, Eldaah. ‘All these were the descendants of Keturah’ (kol-ʾēlleh bênê qĕṭûrāh). The closing formula ends the three-branch Abraham genealogy (Ishmael / Keturah / Isaac). Ephah of Midian appears in Isa 60:6 among the nations that will bring gold and frankincense to Jerusalem — the genealogical root of Ephah in Abraham positions this future ingathering of nations as a homecoming of distant family.
34: wayyôleḏ ʾaḇrāhām ʾet-yiṣḥāq — ‘Abraham fathered Isaac.’ After the extensive Ishmael and Keturah branches, the covenantal line snaps back to a single statement. ‘Sons of Isaac: Esau and Israel’ (ʿêšāw wĕyiśrāʾêl) — the Chronicler uses the covenant name ‘Israel’ rather than ‘Jacob’ throughout, consistent with his practice of preferring the name given in Gen 32:28 / Gen 35:10. Esau is listed first by birth order; Israel second by covenantal priority. The same inversion as Isaac before Ishmael (v28): covenantal priority supersedes birth order throughout this genealogy.
35: Sons of Esau (bênê ʿêšāw): Eliphaz, Reuel, Jeush, Jalam, Korah. Corresponding exactly to Gen 36:4-5. Eliphaz and Reuel are sons of Esau’s Canaanite and Ishmaelite wives respectively. The Esau branch receives more attention (vv35-42) than the Keturah branch, because Edom (Esau’s nation) is the Chronicler’s politically relevant neighbor in the post-exilic period.
36: Sons of Eliphaz (bênê ʾĕlîpaz): Teman, Omar, Zephi, Gatam, Kenaz, Timna, and Amalek. The list differs from Gen 36:12, which lists Timna as a concubine of Eliphaz who bore Amalek — making Timna a mother rather than a sibling. Chronicles places both as Eliphaz’s progeny at the same level. Amalek is the significant name: the Amalekites are Israel’s primordial enemy (Exod 17:8-16; Deut 25:17-19; 1 Sam 15), and their eponymous ancestor is here placed as Eliphaz’s son — Abraham’s great-great-grandson.
37: Sons of Reuel (bênê rĕʿûʾêl): Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, Mizzah — identical to Gen 36:13 (with the same names). Reuel is also the name of Moses’s Midianite father-in-law in Exod 2:18; the shared name is a coincidence of common ancient Near Eastern naming practice, not a genealogical connection.
38: Sons of Seir (bênê śêʿîr): Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah, Dishon, Ezer, Dishan. These are not Esau’s sons but the original Horite inhabitants of the Seir region whom Esau displaced and intermarried with (Gen 36:20-21; Deut 2:12 ‘the Horites formerly lived in Seir’). The Chronicler includes them because the nation Israel knows as Edom is an ethnic blend of Esau’s sons and the indigenous Horite population.
39: Sons of Lotan (bênê lôṭān): Hori and Homam. Lotan’s sister was Timna (wĕʾăĥôt lôṭān timnāʿ). This Timna is the same woman who becomes Eliphaz’s concubine and the mother of Amalek (Gen 36:22). The Chronicler notes the relationship without comment: the intermarriage of Esau’s line with the Seirite Horites produces the Edomite nation.
40: Sons of Shobal (bênê šôḇāl): Alian, Manahath, Ebal, Shephi, Onam. Sons of Zibeon: Aiah and Anah. The Horite clans continue. Manahath appears also in 1 Chr 8:6 as a place-name in Benjamin — the genealogy’s personal names and later territorial names overlap in ways that trace Israel’s own land-distribution back to pre-Israelite inhabitants.
41: Anah’s son: Dishon (bênê ʿānāh dîšôn). Sons of Dishon: Hamran, Eshban, Ithran, Cheran. The list corresponds to Gen 36:26 with minor spelling differences (Hemdan/Hamran). The depth of this Horite genealogy — four generations from Seir — reflects that the Chronicler is tracking the Edomite social structure with some precision.
42: Sons of Ezer (bênê ʾêṣer): Bilhan, Zaavan, Jaakan. Sons of Dishan (bênê dîšān): Uz and Aran. The Ezer/Dishan branch closes the Horite catalogue. Jaakan appears in Num 33:31-32 as a wilderness camping station (Bene-jaakan) — connecting the genealogical eponyms to the physical geography of Israel’s wilderness journey.
43: wĕʾêlleh hammêlāḵîm ʾăšer māleḵû bêʾereṣ ʾeḏôm lipnê meloḵ-melek lêḇenê yiśrāʾêl — ‘These are the kings who reigned in Edom before any king reigned over Israel.’ The temporal clause lipnê meloḵ-melek (before a king reigned) uses the infinitive construct of mālaḵ (to reign as king) twice — the idiom marks the Edomite list as pre-Davidic. For the Chronicler, whose entire narrative centers on the Davidic monarchy, this clause is prospective: it marks the Edomite king-list as temporally prior and implicitly asks “when will Israel have its own king?” The list of Edom’s kings without a permanent dynasty sets up the contrast with the Davidic covenant’s promise of an eternal dynasty.
44: When Bela died, Jobab son of Zerah from Bozrah reigned (wayyimloḵ taḥtāyw, ‘reigned in his place’). The succession formula taḥtāyw (lit. ‘under/in his place’) is the standard idiom for replacement-succession. What is absent is any father-son relation: Jobab is ‘son of Zerah,’ not ‘son of Bela.’ The Edomite kingship is elective or achieved, not hereditary — a stark contrast with the Davidic promise in 2 Sam 7:12 (‘I will raise up your offspring after you’) and 1 Chr 17:11-14.
45: When Jobab died, Husham of the land of the Temanites reigned (wayyimloḵ taḥtāyw ĥûšām mêʾereṣ hatêmānî). Geographic origins replace patronymics throughout this list: kings are identified by where they come from (Bozrah, Temanite land, Moabite territory, Masrekah, Rehoboth-by-the-River), not whose sons they are. Power rotates among regional power bases — the non-dynastic nature of Edomite kingship is built into the formula.
46: Hadad son of Bedad (h˘ḏaḏ ben-bêḏaḏ) reigned after Husham — ‘he had defeated Midian on the territory of Moab’ (hammaккeh ʾet-miḏyān biśĕḏêh môʾāḇ). This is the only king in the list identified by a military achievement. The mention of a Midian-defeat connects the Edomite king-list to the broader geopolitical struggles of the region — the same Midianites who descended from Keturah (v33) and who are Israel’s adversary in the wilderness period.
47: Samlah of Masrekah (śamllāh mimmāśrêqāh) reigned after Hadad. Masrekah is unidentified but the name suggests a wadi-region (the root śrq relates to vines or a winding course). The place-names in this list are drawn from an ancient source that the Chronicler and Gen 36 preserve verbatim; they provide a window into the political geography of Transjordan before the Israelite settlement.
48: Shaul of Rehoboth-by-the-River (šāʾûl mêreĥôḇôt hannāhār) reigned after Samlah. hannāhār (the River) in OT geographical language refers to the Euphrates (cf. Gen 31:21; Exod 23:31). ‘Rehoboth-by-the-Euphrates’ places this king’s origin in northern Syria — evidence that the Edomite royal selection drew from a wide geographic pool, perhaps through trade networks.
49: Baal-hanan son of Achbor (baʿal-ḥānān ben-ʿaḵbôr) reigned after Shaul. The theophoric name Baal-hanan (‘the Lord [Baal] is gracious’) is the only Baal-compound name in the Edomite king-list. In 1 Chr 27:28, a Baal-hanan is David’s overseer of the olive and sycamore groves in the Shephelah — possibly an Edomite or Canaanite official appointed by David, with the name surviving in administrative use.
50: When Baal-hanan died, Hadad reigned (wayyimloḵ taḥtāyw h˘ḏaḏ). His city was Pai (pāʿî; written Pau in Gen 36:39 — a minor spelling variant). His wife was Mehetabel daughter of Matred, daughter of Me-zahab (mêhêṭaḇʾêl bat-maṭrêḏ bat mê-zāhāḇ). The wife’s name and two-generation maternal genealogy is unusual for this list; it is the most detailed entry. mê-zāhāḇ literally means ‘waters of gold’ — possibly a gold-producing region.
51: Hadad also died (wayyāmāt h˘ḏaḏ) — the abrupt death notice without naming a successor signals the end of the king-list and the transition to the chief-list. The chiefs of Edom (ʾallûpê ʾeḏôm): Timna, Aliah, Jetheth. ʾallûp (chief, lit. ‘one of a thousand’) differs from melek (king) — the chiefs are clan-leaders rather than monarchs. The shift from king-list to chief-list may reflect different periods of Edomite social organization.
52: Chief Oholibamah (ʾallûp ʾohôlîḇāmāh), Elah, Pinon. Oholibamah is a female name (the same as Esau’s wife in Gen 36:2) used here as a chief’s name — either the same person or a place-name that became a clan designation. The use of female-associated names for clan chiefs reflects that tribal genealogical identity was often traced through prominent women.
53: Chief Kenaz (ʾallûp qênaz), Teman, Mibzar. Kenaz is also the name of Esau’s grandson (v36: Eliphaz’s son) and the ancestor of Othniel the Kenizzite (Josh 15:17; Judg 3:9 ‘Othniel the son of Kenaz, Caleb’s younger brother’). The Kenizzites are among the pre-Israelite peoples whose land is promised to Abraham’s descendants (Gen 15:19). The overlap between the Edomite chief Kenaz and Israelite judge Othniel’s lineage reflects the complex ethnic interweaving of Israelite and Edomite ancestry in the Negev region.
54: Chief Magdiel (ʾallûp magdîʾêl) and chief Iram. ‘These were the chiefs of Edom’ (ʾêlleh ʾallûpê ʾeḏôm) — the closing formula ends the Edomite section and closes chapter 1 entirely. The Chronicler has moved from Adam (v1) to the post-Abrahamic nations, demonstrating that Israel and Judah stand within, not apart from, the full human genealogical matrix. The chapter is simultaneously universal (beginning at Adam) and particular (closing on Edom, Israel’s near-neighbor and adversary) before the Chronicler turns in chapter 2 to Israel’s own tribes.
